# Sacratos

> Sacratos is a documentary photography and storytelling project that captures the world's most hidden and dangerous cultural events. Founded by Daniele Colucci and Mattia Astori, Sacratos travels to places that don't show up on maps or Instagram feeds — following word of mouth, sleeping wherever welcomed, and documenting chaos, beauty, and raw devotion.

## About

- Name: Sacratos
- Tagline: Stories of Raw Devotion
- Website: https://sacratos.com
- Founded: 2024
- Reach: 115M+ views across platforms
- Featured by: Wikipedia, Insta360, All About Photo

## Team

- **Daniele Colucci** — Co-Creator, Photographer, Videomaker (https://danielecolucci.com)
- **Mattia Astori** — Co-Creator, Producer, Photographer (https://mattiaastori.com)

## What We Do

We travel to the world's most hidden and dangerous cultural events. No hotels, no crew, one backpack. We follow the trail of stories, sleeping wherever we're welcomed — someone's couch, a tent, the back of a truck. Our trips are built on word of mouth. Locals point us to someone who knows someone. That's how we find the things no one talks about.

These aren't polished documentaries. They are raw, first-person accounts from festivals and rituals that mainstream media misses.

## Stories & Events Documented

### Feria Internacional de la Pirotecnia — Tultepec, Mexico
200 bulls loaded with rockets are thrown against thousands of people. 53 wounded. One of the world's most dangerous fireworks festivals.

### Banni Festival — India
A midnight battlefield where bloodshed is considered a sacred offering.

### Maut Ka Kuan (Well of Death) — India
Riders drive motorcycles on vertical wooden walls in the "Well of Death." Featuring the story of Soma Basu, a Bengali woman stunt rider who defied tradition. Published story: "Riding the Well of Death: Soma Basu's Defiance of Gravity and Tradition."

### Vegetarian Festival — Thailand
Spirit mediums pierce their skin in rituals believed to ward off evil.

### Exploding Hammers — San Juan de la Vega, Mexico
Hammers packed with explosives are slammed against stone anvils.

### Tinku de Macha — Bolivia
Day 1: Dance. Day 2: Fight. Day 3: Mourn. At 4,000 meters altitude, blood seals a harvest year.

## Topics & Expertise

Documentary photography, photojournalism, cultural anthropology, dangerous festivals, hidden rituals, world travel, adventure documentary, immersive storytelling, Mexican festivals, Indian festivals, Thai rituals, Bolivian traditions, fireworks festivals, stunt culture, spirit mediums, extreme cultural events
